revision.text:
[[ast:Sistema xaponés d'unidaes]][[be_x_old:Японская сыстэма мер]][[ca:Sistema japonès de mesures]][[de:Shakkanhō]][[en:Japanese units of measurement]][[fa:واحدهای اندازه‌گیری ژاپنی]][[fr:Unité de mesure japonaise]][[hi:जापानी मापन इकाइयाँ]][[hu:Japán mértékegységrendszer]][[it:Unità di misura giapponesi]][[ja:尺貫法]][[ko:척관법]][[pl:Dawny japoński system miar i wag]][[pt:Unidades de medida japonesas]][[ru:Японская система мер]][[th:มาตราชั่งตวงวัดของญี่ปุ่น]][[uk:Японська система мір]][[zh:尺貫法]] {{Short description|Traditional system of measurement used in Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=August 2021}} {{more citations needed|date=June 2018}} Traditional '''Japanese units of measurement''' or the '''shakkanhō''' ({{lang|ja|尺貫法}}) is the [[:en:Customary units|traditional]] [[:en:System of measurement|System of measurement]] used by the people of the [[Japanese archipelago]]. It is largely based on the {{ill|Chinese units of measurement|en|Chinese units of measurement|qid=Q435922|lt=Chinese system|1=Chinese units of measurement}}, which spread to Japan and the rest of the {{ill|Sinosphere|en|Sinosphere|qid=Q716566|lt=Sinosphere|1=Sinosphere}} in antiquity. It has remained mostly unaltered since the adoption of the measures of the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|qid=Q9683|lt=Tang dynasty|1=Tang dynasty}} in 701.{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%B0%BA%E8%B2%AB%E6%B3%95-76054|title=尺貫法(しゃっかんほう)とは|website=コトバンク|language=ja|access-date=30 September 2019}} Following the 1868 [[Meiji Restoration]], [[:en:Imperial Japan|Imperial Japan]] {{ill|Metrication|en|Metrication|qid=Q2738925|lt=adopted|1=Metrication}} the {{ill|Metric system|en|Metric system|qid=Q232405|lt=Metric system|1=Metric system}} and defined the traditional units in metric terms on the basis of a [[:en:Prototype metre|Prototype metre]] and [[:en:Prototype kilogram|kilogram]]. The present values of most [[:en:Korean units|Korean]] and {{ill|Taiwanese units of measurement|en|Taiwanese units of measurement|qid=Q715103|lt=Taiwanese units of measurement|1=Taiwanese units of measurement}} derive from these values as well. For a time in the early 20th century, the traditional, metric, and {{ill|English units|en|English units|qid=Q136513724|lt=English systems|1=English units}} were all legal in Japan. Although commerce has since been legally restricted to using the metric system, the old system is still used in some instances. The old measures are common in {{ill|Japanese carpentry|en|Japanese carpentry|qid=Q10239892|lt=carpentry|1=Japanese carpentry}} and [[:en:Japanese agriculture|agriculture]], with tools such as {{ill|Japanese chisel|en|Japanese chisel|qid=Q20982924|lt=chisel|1=Japanese chisel}}s, spatels, saws, and hammers manufactured in ''sun'' and ''bu'' sizes. [[:en:Floorspace|Floorspace]] is expressed in terms of [[:en:Tatami mat|Tatami mat]]s, and land is sold on the basis of price in ''tsubo''. [[Sake]] is sold in multiples of 1{{nbsp}}''[[:en:Gō (unit)|gō]]'', with the most common bottle sizes being 4 (720 mL) or 10 (1.8 L, ''isshōbin'').{{Cite web|url=https://www.hakushika.co.jp/en/culture/measurements.html|title = Hakushika | Sake Culture | Sake and Traditional Japanese Measurements}} ==History== Customary Japanese units are a local adaption of the [[:en:Chinese units|traditional Chinese system]], which was adopted at a very early date. They were imposed and adjusted at various times by local and imperial statutes. The details of the system have varied over time and location in Japan's history.{{cite book |last1=Iwata |first1=Shigeo |chapter=Weights and Measures in Japan |title=Encyclopaedia of the History of Science, Technology, and Medicine in Non-Western Cultures |year=2008 |pages=2267–2271 |doi=10.1007/978-1-4020-4425-0_8935 |isbn=978-1-4020-4559-2 |chapter-url=https://link.springer.com/referenceworkentry/10.1007/978-1-4020-4425-0_8935#Bib1_8935}} Japan signed the [[:en:Treaty of the Metre|Treaty of the Metre]] in 1885, with its terms taking effect in 1886. It received its [[:en:Prototype metre|Prototype metre]] and [[:en:Prototype kilogram|kilogram]] from the {{ill|International Bureau of Weights and Measures|en|International Bureau of Weights and Measures|qid=Q229478|lt=International Bureau of Weights and Measures|1=International Bureau of Weights and Measures}} in 1890. The next year, a weights and measurements law codified the Japanese system, taking its fundamental units to be the ''shaku'' and ''kan'' and deriving the others from them. The law codified the values of the traditional and metric units in terms of one another, but retained the traditional units as the formal standard and metric values as secondary.{{sfnp|Lyon|1902|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=yShJAQAAIAAJ&pg=PA933 933]}} {| class=wikitable |+ 1891 definitions ! scope="col" colspan="2"|Unit ! scope="col" rowspan="2"|Definition ! scope="col" rowspan="2" colspan="4"|Conversions |- !{{ill|Hepburn romanization|en|Hepburn romanization|qid=Q667558|lt=Romanised|1=Hepburn romanization}} !{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|qid=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}} |- !colspan="2"|[[#Length|Length]] !metres !metres ! colspan=2 | [[:en:US foot|feet]] |- style="text-align: right" ! scope="row" align="center"| ''{{ill|Shaku (unit)|en|Shaku (unit)|qid=Q931805|lt=shaku|1=Shaku (unit)}}'' |align="center"| {{lang|ja|尺}} |align="center" height="30pt"| {{frac|10|33}} | 0.303 | colspan=2 | 0.9942 |- !colspan="2"|[[#Area|Area]] !square
metres ! square
metres ! colspan=2 | square
feet |- style="text-align: right" ! scope="row" align="center"| ''[[:en:Tsubo|Tsubo]]'' |align="center"| {{lang|ja|坪}} |align="center" height="30pt"| {{frac|100|30.25}} | 3.306 | colspan=2 | 35.58 |- !colspan="2"|[[#Volume|Volume]] !litres !litres !{{ill|US gallon|en|US gallon|qid=Q23925413|lt=US
gallons|1=US gallon}} !{{ill|Imperial gallon|en|Imperial gallon|qid=Q23925410|lt=Imperial
gallons|1=Imperial gallon}} |- style="text-align: right" ! scope="row" align="center"| ''shō'' |align="center"| {{lang|ja|升}} |align="center" height="30pt"| {{frac|2401|1331}} | 1.804 | 0.4765 | 0.3968 |- !colspan="2"|[[#Mass|Mass]] !kilograms !kilograms ! colspan=2 | {{ill|Pound (mass)|en|Pound (mass)|qid=Q100995|lt=pound|1=Pound (mass)}}s |- style="text-align: right" ! scope="row" align="center"| ''kan'' |align="center"| {{lang|ja|貫}} |align="center" height="30pt"| {{frac|15|4}} | 3.750 | colspan=2 | 8.267 |- | colspan="13" bgcolor=#F0F0F0|'''Note:''' Definitions are exact and conversions are rounded to four significant figures. |} In 1909, {{ill|English units|en|English units|qid=Q136513724|lt=English units|1=English units}} were also made legal within the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan|1=Empire of Japan}}. Following [[:en:Japan in World War I|World War I]], the {{ill|Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce|en|Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce|qid=Q3315480|lt=Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce|1=Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce}} established a Committee for Weights and Measures and Industrial Standards, part of whose remit was to investigate which of Japan's three legal systems should be adopted. Upon its advice, the Imperial Diet established the metric system as Japan's legal standard, effective 1 July 1924,{{citation |contribution=改正度量衡法規 |contribution-url=http://kindai.da.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/796077 |title=Digital Collections |url=http://dl.ndl.go.jp/?__lang=en |location=Tokyo |publisher=National Diet Library }}. {{in lang|ja}} with use of the other systems permitted as a transitional measure. The government and "leading industries" were to convert within the next decade, with others following in the decade after that.{{harvp|Tamano|1971|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=dFLL50miMpgC&pg=PA98 98]}}. {{ill|Education in Japan|en|Education in Japan|qid=Q834934|lt=Public education|1=Education in Japan}}—at the time compulsory through primary school—began to teach the metric system. Governmental agencies and the Japanese Weights and Measures Association undertook a gradual course of education and conversion but opposition became vehemently outspoken in the early 1930s. {{ill|Japanese nationalism|en|Japanese nationalism|qid=Q1331440|lt=Nationalist|1=Japanese nationalism}}s decried the "foreign" system as harmful to Japanese pride, language, and culture, as well as restrictive to international trade. In 1933, the government pushed the deadline for the conversion of the first group of industries to 1939; the rest of the country was given until 1954. Emboldened, the nationalists succeeded in having an Investigating Committee for Weights and Measures Systems established. In 1938, it advised that the government should continue to employ the "Shaku–Kan" system alongside the metric one. The next year, the imperial ordinance concerning the transition to the metric system was formally revised, indefinitely exempting real estate and historical objects and treasures from any need for metric conversion. The deadline for compulsory conversion in all other fields was moved back to 31 December 1958. Following {{ill|Surrender of Japan|en|Surrender of Japan|qid=Q6540361|lt=its defeat|1=Surrender of Japan}} in {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=World War II|1=Pacific War}}, Japan was {{ill|Occupation of Japan|en|Occupation of Japan|qid=Q696251|lt=occupied by America|1=Occupation of Japan}} and saw an expanded use of [[:en:US customary units|US customary units]]. Gasoline was sold by the {{ill|Gallon|en|Gallon|qid=Q178413|lt=Gallon|1=Gallon}} and cloth by the yard.{{harvp|Tamano|1971|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=dFLL50miMpgC&pg=PA99 99]}}. The Diet revisited the nation's measurements and, with the occupation's approval, promulgated a Measurements Law in June 1951 that reaffirmed its intention to continue Japan's metrication, effective on the first day of 1959. An unofficial and ''ad hoc'' Metric System Promotion Committee was established by interested scholars, public servants, and businessmen in August 1955, undertaking a public awareness campaign and seeking to accomplish as much of the conversion ahead of schedule as possible. Its first success was the conversion of candy sales in Tokyo department stores from the ''momme'' to the gram in September 1956; others followed, with {{ill|NHK|en|NHK|qid=Q212128|lt=NHK|1=NHK}} taking the lead in media use.{{harvp|Tamano|1971|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=dFLL50miMpgC&pg=PA100 100]}}. With the majority of the public now exposed to it since childhood, the metric system became the sole legal measurement system in most fields of Japanese life on 1 January 1959.{{harvp|Tamano|1971|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=dFLL50miMpgC&pg=PA97 97]}}. Redrafting of laws to use metric equivalents had already been accomplished, but conversion of the [[:en:Land registry|land registries]] required until 31 March 1966 to complete.{{citation |contribution=メートル条約 |publisher=International Metrology Cooperation Office |contribution-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110722110749/http://www.intermet.jp/metric/ |url=http://www.intermet.jp/en/ |title=''Official site'' |location=Ibaraki |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120309141632/http://www.intermet.jp/en/ |archive-date=9 March 2012 }}. {{in lang|ja}} Industry transitioned gradually at its own expense, with compliance sometimes being nominal, as in the case of {{convert|1/4|in|2|adj=on|lk=on}} screws becoming "{{frac|1|4}} screws".{{harvp|Tamano|1971|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=dFLL50miMpgC&pg=PA101 101]}}. Since the original fines for noncompliance were around [[:en:USD|$]]140 and governmental agencies mostly preferred to wait for voluntary conversion, metric use by December 1959 was estimated at only 85%.{{harvp|Tamano|1971|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=dFLL50miMpgC&pg=PA102 102]}}. Since research showed that individual Japanese did not intend to actually use the metric units when given other options, however, sale and verification of devices marked with non-metric units (such as rulers and tape measures noting ''shaku'' and ''sun'') were criminalised after 1961. Some use of the traditional units continues. Some Japanese describe their weight in terms of ''kan''. Homes continue to be reckoned in terms of ''tsubo'', even on the [[:en:Japanese census|national census]] as late as 2005, although the practice was discontinued in 2010. [[:en:English unit|English unit]]s continue to be employed in aviation, [[munitions]], and various sports, including {{ill|Golf|en|Golf|qid=Q5377|lt=Golf|1=Golf}} and [[:en:Japanese baseball|baseball]]. == Length == [[File:P1239148 Japan Deutschland (Klangwerkstatt) (48281353057).jpg|thumb|A man playing the ''[[shakuhachi]]'' flute, named after its traditional length of 1 ''shaku'' and 8 ''sun'' (54.5 cm)]] The base unit of Japanese length is the ''shaku'' based upon the Chinese ''{{ill|Chi (unit)|en|Chi (unit)|qid=Q2963205|lt=chi|1=Chi (unit)}}'', with other units derived from it and changing over time based on its dimensions. The ''chi'' was originally a {{ill|Span (unit)|en|Span (unit)|qid=Q185759|lt=span|1=Span (unit)}} taken from the end of the thumb to the tip of an outstretched middlefinger, but which gradually increased in length to about {{convert|1/3|m|cm|lk=on}}, just a few centimetres longer than the size of a [[:en:US foot|foot]]. As in China and Korea, Japan employed different ''shaku'' for different purposes. The "carpentry" ''shaku'' ({{lang|ja|曲尺}}, ''kanejaku'') was used for construction. It was a little longer in the 19th century prior to its metric redefinition.{{efn|In the 10th lunar month of the 3rd year of {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji|1=Meiji era}} (around November 1871), the {{ill|Ministry of Railways (Japan)|en|Ministry of Railways (Japan)|qid=Q249654|lt=Railways Ministry|1=Ministry of Railways (Japan)}} defined the "[[:en:US foot|English foot]]" as 1 ''shaku'' 4 ''rin'', making the ''shaku'' equivalent to about 0.996{{nbsp}}ft.{{citation |title=''日本鉄道史 [''Nippon Tetsudō-shi'', ''Japanese Railway History''], Vol. I'' |publisher=Ministry of Railways |location=Tokyo |date=1921 |page=49 }}. {{in lang|ja}}}} The "cloth" or "whale" ''shaku'' ({{lang|ja|鯨尺}}, ''kujirajaku''), named for tailors' and fabric merchants' {{ill|Baleen|en|Baleen|qid=Q275426|lt=Baleen|1=Baleen}} rulers, was {{frac|1|4}} longer and used in measuring cloth. (A longer unit of about 25{{nbsp}}cloth ''shaku'' was the ''tan''.) [[:en:Traditional Japanese clothing|Traditional Japanese clothing]] was reckoned using the "traditional clothing" ''shaku'' ({{lang|zh|呉服尺}}, ''gofukujaku''), about {{frac|5}} longer than the carpentry ''shaku''. The [[:en:Shosoin|Shōsōin]] in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|qid=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}} has ivory 1-''shaku'' rulers, the {{nihongo|''kōgebachiru-no-shaku''|紅牙撥鏤尺|}}. The Japanese ri is now much longer than the {{ill|Li (unit)|en|Li (unit)|qid=Q654875|lt=Chinese|1=Li (unit)}} or [[:en:Korean li|Korean li]], comprising 36 ''chō'', 2160 ''ken'',{{harvp|Renouard|1845|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=LDxOAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA486 486]}}. or 12,960{{nbsp}}''shaku''. A still longer unit was formerly standard in {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|qid=Q864166|lt=Ise|1=Ise Province}} on [[Honshu]] and throughout the {{ill|Saikaidō|en|Saikaidō|qid=Q1334003|lt=9 provinces|1=Saikaidō}} of [[Kyushu]], which comprised 50 ''chō'', 3000 ''ken'', or 18,000{{nbsp}}''shaku''. The [[:en:Imperial nautical mile|Imperial nautical mile]] of 6080{{nbsp}}[[:en:Imperial feet|feet]] (1853.19{{nbsp}}m) was also formerly used by the Japanese in maritime contexts as a "marine ri".{{harvp|USWD|1944|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=-s4DDAAAQBAJ&pg=PA400 400]}}. A fourth and shorter ri of about 600{{nbsp}}m is still evident in some beach names. The "99-Ri" beach at Kujukuri is about 60 km. The "7-Ri" beach at Shichiri is 4.2 km long. {| class=wikitable |+ Table of Lengths[https://books.google.com/books?id=kt9DIY1g9HYC&dq=%22conversion+coefficients+between+these+two%22&pg=PA2270 Iwata, Shigeo. "Weights and Measures in Japan"] !colspan="2"|Unit !rowspan="2"|{{ill|Shaku (unit)|en|Shaku (unit)|qid=Q931805|lt=Shaku|1=Shaku (unit)}} !colspan="2"| {{ill|Metric system|en|Metric system|qid=Q232405|lt=Metric|1=Metric system}} !colspan="2" | [[:en:US customary units|US]] & {{ill|Imperial units|en|Imperial units|qid=Q641227|lt=Imperial|1=Imperial units}} |- !{{ill|Hepburn romanization|en|Hepburn romanization|qid=Q667558|lt=Romanised|1=Hepburn romanization}} !{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|qid=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}} !Exact !Approx. !Exact !Approx. |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | Mō |align="center"| {{lang|ja|毛}} or {{lang|ja|毫}} |height="30pt"|  {{frac|10000}}  | {{sfrac|1|33,000}}{{nbsp}}m | 0.03030{{nbsp}}mm | {{sfrac|5|150,876}}{{nbsp}}yd | 0.001193{{nbsp}}in |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | Rin |align="center"| {{lang|ja|厘}} or {{lang|ja|釐}} |height="30pt"| {{frac|1000}}  | {{sfrac|1|3300}}{{nbsp}}m | 0.3030{{nbsp}}mm | {{sfrac|25|75,438}}{{nbsp}}yd | 0.01193{{nbsp}}in |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | Bu |align="center"| {{lang|ja|分}} |height="30pt"| {{frac|100}}  | {{sfrac|1|330}}{{nbsp}}m | 3.030{{nbsp}}mm | {{sfrac|125|37,719}}{{nbsp}}yd | 0.1193{{nbsp}}in |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | {{ill|Cun (unit)|en|Cun (unit)|qid=Q876237|lt=Sun|1=Cun (unit)}} |align="center"| {{lang|ja|寸}} |height="30pt"| {{frac|10}}  | {{sfrac|1|33}}{{nbsp}}m | 3.030{{nbsp}}cm | {{sfrac|1250|37,719}}{{nbsp}}yd | 1.193{{nbsp}}in |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | {{ill|Shaku (unit)|en|Shaku (unit)|qid=Q931805|lt=Shaku|1=Shaku (unit)}} |align="center"| {{lang|ja|尺}} | 1  |  {{sfrac|10|33}}{{nbsp}}m | 30.30{{nbsp}}cm | {{sfrac|12,500|37,719}}{{nbsp}}yd | 11.93{{nbsp}}in |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | {{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|qid=Q3195007|lt=Ken|1=Ken (unit)}}{{efn|The ken{{sfn|''OED''|loc=[http://www.oed.com/view/Entry/102888 "ken, ''n.³''"]}} is also found in English sources as the ''kin'' or glossed as the Japanese [[:en:Fathom (unit)|fathom]].}} |align="center"| {{lang|ja|間}} | rowspan=2 | 6  | rowspan=2 |  {{sfrac|20|11}}{{nbsp}}m | rowspan=2 | 1.818{{nbsp}}m | rowspan=2 | {{sfrac|25,000|12,573}}{{nbsp}}yd | rowspan=2 | 5{{nbsp}}ft{{nbsp}}11.6{{nbsp}}in |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | Hiro |align="center"| {{lang|ja|尋}} |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | Jō |align="center"| {{lang|ja|丈}} | 10  |  {{sfrac|100|33}}{{nbsp}}m | 3.030{{nbsp}}m | {{sfrac|125,000|37,719}}{{nbsp}}yd | 9{{nbsp}}ft{{nbsp}}11.3{{nbsp}}in |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | Chō |align="center"| {{lang|ja|町}} | 360  |  {{sfrac|1200|11}}{{nbsp}}m | 109.1{{nbsp}}m | {{sfrac|500,000|4191}}{{nbsp}}yd | 357{{nbsp}}ft{{nbsp}}11{{nbsp}}in |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | [[:en:Ri (unit)|Ri]]{{efn|The Japanese ''ri'' is also frequently known by its Chinese name li or glossed as the Japanese mile.{{harvp|Renouard|1845|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=LDxOAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA490 490]}}.}} |align="center"| {{lang|ja|里}} | 12,960  | {{sfrac|43,200|11}}{{nbsp}}m | 3.927{{nbsp}}km | {{sfrac|6,000,000|1397}}{{nbsp}}yd | 2.440{{nbsp}}mi |- |colspan="13" bgcolor=#F0F0F0|'''Notes:''' *Exact figures follow the 1891 Law of Weights & Measures and 1959 International Yard and Pound Agreement. *Approximations are rounded to four {{ill|Significant figures|en|Significant figures|qid=Q1056761|lt=Significant figures|1=Significant figures}}. *The names of the smallest units, borrowed from Chinese, also function as the Japanese names
for the fractions "hundredth", "thousandth", and "ten-thousandth". *The ken is the normal unit of distance and length; the ''hiro'' used as the equivalent unit of depth. *Sometimes ''hiro'' is used equal to 5{{nbsp}}''shaku''{{cite journal |last1=Matsui |first1=Tetsuhiro |title=Is length of "Hiro" five shakus or six? About the length unit "Hiro" in Japanese classic boat documents. |journal=Research on the History of Metrology |date=2007-01-10 |volume=29 |issue=1 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/10632121}} ({{sfrac|50|33}}{{nbsp}}m, ~1.5152 metres). |} The traditional units are still used for construction materials in Japan. For example, plywood is usually manufactured in {{nowrap|182 cm × 91 cm}} (about {{nowrap|72 in × 36 in}}) sheets known in the trade as {{nihongo|''saburokuhan''|3 × 6版|}}, or 3 × 6 ''shaku''. Each sheet is about the size of one [[tatami]] mat. The thicknesses of the sheets, however, are usually measured in millimetres. The names of these units also live in the name of the bamboo flute {{nihongo|[[shakuhachi]]|尺八|}}, literally "shaku eight", which measures one ''shaku'' and eight ''sun'', and the Japanese version of the Tom Thumb story, {{nihongo|[[:en:One-inch boy|Issun Bōshi]]|一寸法師|}}, literally "one ''sun'' boy", as well as in many {{ill|Japanese proverbs|en|Japanese proverbs|qid=Q1149363|lt=Japanese proverbs|1=Japanese proverbs}}. == Area == [[File:Anforet_3F_2017-06-02_ac_(2).jpg|thumb|Area floored with 8 tatami mats (4 ''tsubo'', or 8 ''jō'')]] The base unit of Japanese area is the ''[[:en:Tsubo|Tsubo]]'', equivalent to a square {{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|qid=Q3195007|lt=ken|1=Ken (unit)}} or 36 square ''shaku''. It is twice the size of the ''jō'', the area of the Nagoya [[tatami]] mat. Both units are used informally in discussing real estate [[:en:Floorspace|Floorspace]].{{cite book |last1=Gyllenbok |first1=Jan |title=Encyclopaedia of Historical Metrology, Weights and Measures, Vol. 1 |series=Science Networks. Historical Studies |date=2018 |volume=56 |page=356 |doi=10.1007/978-3-319-57598-8 |isbn=978-3-319-57596-4 |url=https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-3-319-57598-8}} Tsubo is a square ken. Gyllenbok says that the size of tatami or jo became “standardized in the Muromachi Period (1338–1573) at one ken long and half a ken wide. The ken, however, has varied over the centuries. It is now generally about 1.82m, but reaches 1.97m in the Kansai area, including the cities of Kyoto, Osaka, and Kobe. Thus, one tatami is about 1.62 to 1.95m.” Due to {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|qid=Q137816|lt=historical connections|1=Taiwan under Japanese rule}}, the tsubo is still used as the official base unit of [[Taiwanese units of measurement#Area|area in Taiwan]]. In agricultural contexts, the ''tsubo'' is known as the ''bu''. The larger units remain in common use by Japanese farmers when discussing the sizes of fields. {| class="wikitable" |+ Table of area units !colspan="2"|Unit !rowspan="2"|[[:en:Tsubo|Tsubo]] !colspan="2"|{{ill|Metric system|en|Metric system|qid=Q232405|lt=Metric|1=Metric system}} !colspan="3"|[[:en:US customary units|US]] & {{ill|Imperial units|en|Imperial units|qid=Q641227|lt=Imperial|1=Imperial units}} |- !{{ill|Hepburn romanization|en|Hepburn romanization|qid=Q667558|lt=Romanized|1=Hepburn romanization}} !{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|qid=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}} !Exact !Approx. !Exact !Approx. |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | Shaku |align="center"|{{lang|ja|勺}} |height="30pt"| {{frac|100}} | {{sfrac|4|121}}{{nbsp}}m2 | 330.6{{nbsp}}cm2 | {{sfrac|6,250,000|158,080,329}}{{nbsp}}sq{{nbsp}}yd | 51.24{{nbsp}}sq{{nbsp}}in |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | Gō |align="center"| {{lang|ja|合}} |height="30pt"| {{frac|10}} | {{sfrac|40|121}}{{nbsp}}m2 | 0.3306{{nbsp}}m2 | {{sfrac|62,500,000|158,080,329}}{{nbsp}}sq{{nbsp}}yd | 3.558{{nbsp}}sq{{nbsp}}ft |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | Jō |align="center"| {{lang|ja|畳}} or {{lang|ja|帖}} |height="30pt"| {{frac|2}} | {{sfrac|200|121}}{{nbsp}}m2 | 1.653{{nbsp}}m2 | {{sfrac|312,500,000|158,080,329}}{{nbsp}}sq{{nbsp}}yd | 17.79{{nbsp}}sq{{nbsp}}ft |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | [[:en:Tsubo|Tsubo]] | align="center" | {{lang|ja|坪}} | rowspan=2 | 1 | rowspan=2 | {{sfrac|400|121}}{{nbsp}}m2 | rowspan=2 | 3.306{{nbsp}}m2 | rowspan=2 | {{sfrac|625,000,000|158,080,329}}{{nbsp}}sq{{nbsp}}yd | rowspan=2 | 35.58{{nbsp}}sq{{nbsp}}ft |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | Bu |align="center"| {{lang|ja|歩}} |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | Se |align="center"| {{lang|ja|畝}} | 30 | {{sfrac|12,000|121}}{{nbsp}}m2 | 99.17{{nbsp}}m2 | {{sfrac|6,250,000,000|52,693,443}}{{nbsp}}sq{{nbsp}}yd | 1,067{{nbsp}}sq{{nbsp}}ft |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | Tan |align="center"| {{lang|ja|段}} or {{lang|ja|反}} | 300 | {{sfrac|120,000|121}}{{nbsp}}m2 | 991.7{{nbsp}}m2 | {{sfrac|62,500,000,000|52,693,443}}{{nbsp}}sq{{nbsp}}yd | 10,674.6{{nbsp}}sq{{nbsp}}ft |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | Chō(bu){{efn|''Chōbu'' is used rather than ''chō'' when no fraction follows.}} |align="center"| {{lang|ja|町}}({{lang|ja|}}) | 3000 | {{sfrac|1,200,000|121}}{{nbsp}}m2 | 0.9917{{nbsp}}ha | {{sfrac|625,000,000,000|52,693,443}}{{nbsp}}sq{{nbsp}}yd | 2.4505{{nbsp}}acres |- |colspan="13" bgcolor=#F0F0F0|'''Notes:''' *Approximations are rounded to four significant figures. |} == Volume == [[File:甲午 (11711782255).jpg|thumb|A wooden ''[[Masu (measurement)|masu]]'' sake cup (1 ''gō'') for celebrations]] The base unit of Japanese volume is the ''shō'', although the ''[[:en:Gō (unit)|gō]]'' now sees more use since it is reckoned as the appropriate size of a {{ill|Serving size|en|Serving size|qid=Q7455897|lt=serving|1=Serving size}} of rice or [[sake]]. Sake and [[:en:Shochu|Shochu]] are both commonly sold in large 1800{{nbsp}}mL bottles known as {{nihongo|''isshōbin''|一升瓶}}, literally "one ''shō'' bottle".{{Cite web|url=https://www.urbansake.com/sake-101/sake-glossary/isshobin/|title=Isshobin | 一升瓶|website=UrbanSake.com}} The ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|qid=Q855048|lt=Koku|1=Koku}}'' is historically important: since it was reckoned as the amount of rice necessary to feed a person for a single year, it was used to compute agricultural output and official salaries. The ''koku'' of rice was sometimes reckoned as 3000{{nbsp}}"sacks". By the 1940s the shipping ''koku'' was {{frac|10}} of the {{ill|Shipping ton|en|Shipping ton|qid=Q25303761|lt=Shipping ton|1=Shipping ton}} of 40 or 42{{nbsp}}cu{{nbsp}}ft (i.e., {{convert|4|-|4.2|cuft|L|disp=out}}); the ''koku'' of timber was about 10{{nbsp}}cu{{nbsp}}ft ({{convert|10|cuft|L|disp=out}}); and the ''koku'' of fish, like many modern {{ill|Bushel|en|Bushel|qid=Q216658|lt=Bushel|1=Bushel}}s, was no longer reckoned by volume but computed by weight (40{{nbsp}}''kan''). The ''shakujime'' of timber was about 12{{nbsp}}cu{{nbsp}}ft ({{convert|12|cuft|L|disp=out}}) and the ''taba'' about 108{{nbsp}}ft³ ({{convert|108|cuft|L|disp=out}} or {{convert|108|cuft|m3|disp=out}}). {| class="wikitable" |+ Table of volume units !colspan="2"|Unit !rowspan="2"|Shō !colspan="2"|{{ill|Metric system|en|Metric system|qid=Q232405|lt=Metric|1=Metric system}} !colspan="2"|[[United States customary units#Fluid volume|US]] !colspan="3"|{{ill|Imperial units|en|Imperial units|qid=Q641227|lt=Imperial|1=Imperial units}} |- !{{ill|Hepburn romanization|en|Hepburn romanization|qid=Q667558|lt=Romanized|1=Hepburn romanization}} !{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|qid=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}} !Exact !Approx. !Exact !Approx. !Exact !Approx. |- | rowspan=2 | 1.804{{nbsp}}mL | rowspan=2 | {{sfrac|37,515,625|15,900,351,812,136}}{{nbsp}}cu{{nbsp}}yd | 29.28{{nbsp}}[[:en:US minim|min]] | rowspan=2 | {{sfrac|240,100|605,084,579}}{{nbsp}}{{ill|Imperial gallon|en|Imperial gallon|qid=Q23925410|lt=gal|1=Imperial gallon}} | rowspan=2 | 30.47{{nbsp}}[[:en:Imperial minim|min]] |- style="text-align: right" | 0.1101{{nbsp}}cu{{nbsp}}in |- style="text-align: right" | rowspan=2 style="text-align:left;" | Shaku | rowspan=2 align="center"| {{lang|ja|勺}} | rowspan=2 height="30pt"| {{frac|100}} | rowspan=2 | {{sfrac|2401|133,100}}{{nbsp}}L | rowspan=2 | 18.04{{nbsp}}mL | rowspan=2 | {{sfrac|187,578,125|7,950,175,906,068}}{{nbsp}}cu{{nbsp}}yd | 0.6100{{nbsp}}[[:en:US fluid ounce|fl{{nbsp}}oz]] | rowspan=2 | {{sfrac|2,401,000|605,084,579}}{{nbsp}}{{ill|Imperial gallon|en|Imperial gallon|qid=Q23925410|lt=gal|1=Imperial gallon}} | rowspan=2 | 0.6349{{nbsp}}[[:en:Imperial fluid ounce|fl{{nbsp}}oz]] |- style="text-align: right" | 1.101{{nbsp}}cu{{nbsp}}in |- style="text-align: right" | rowspan=2 style="text-align:left;" | [[:en:Gō (unit)|Gō]] | rowspan=2 align="center"| {{lang|ja|合}} | rowspan=2 height="30pt"| {{frac|10}} | rowspan=2 | {{sfrac|2401|13,310}}{{nbsp}}L | rowspan=2 | 180.4{{nbsp}}mL | rowspan=2 | {{sfrac|937,890,625|3,975,087,953,034}}{{nbsp}}cu{{nbsp}}yd | 0.3812{{nbsp}}[[:en:US liquid pint|pt]] | rowspan=2 | {{sfrac|24,010,000|605,084,579}}{{nbsp}}{{ill|Imperial gallon|en|Imperial gallon|qid=Q23925410|lt=gal|1=Imperial gallon}} | rowspan=2 | 0.3174{{nbsp}}[[:en:Imperial pint|pt]] |- style="text-align: right" | 0.3276{{nbsp}}[[:en:US dry pint|dry{{nbsp}}pt]] |- style="text-align: right" | rowspan=2 style="text-align:left;" | Shō | rowspan=2 align="center"| {{lang|ja|升}} | rowspan=2 | 1 | rowspan=2 | {{sfrac|2401|1331}}{{nbsp}}L | rowspan=2 | 1.804{{nbsp}}L | rowspan=2 | {{sfrac|4,689,453,125|1,987,543,976,517}}{{nbsp}}cu{{nbsp}}yd | 1.906{{nbsp}}[[:en:US liquid quart|qt]] | rowspan=2 | {{sfrac|240,100,000|605,084,579}}{{nbsp}}{{ill|Imperial gallon|en|Imperial gallon|qid=Q23925410|lt=gal|1=Imperial gallon}} | rowspan=2 | 1.587{{nbsp}}[[:en:Imperial quart|qt]] |- style="text-align: right" | 1.638{{nbsp}}[[:en:US dry quart|dry{{nbsp}}qt]] |- style="text-align: right" | rowspan=2 style="text-align:left;" | To | rowspan=2 align="center"| {{lang|ja|斗}} | rowspan=2 | 10 | rowspan=2 | {{sfrac|24,010|1331}}{{nbsp}}L | rowspan=2 | 18.04{{nbsp}}L | rowspan=2 | {{sfrac|46,894,531,250|1,987,543,976,517}}{{nbsp}}cu{{nbsp}}yd | 4.765{{nbsp}}[[:en:US liquid gallon|gal]] | rowspan=2 | {{sfrac|2,401,000,000|605,084,579}}{{nbsp}}{{ill|Imperial gallon|en|Imperial gallon|qid=Q23925410|lt=gal|1=Imperial gallon}} | rowspan=2 | 3.968{{nbsp}}{{ill|Imperial gallon|en|Imperial gallon|qid=Q23925410|lt=gal|1=Imperial gallon}} |- style="text-align: right" | 2.048{{nbsp}}[[:en:US peck|pk]] |- style="text-align: right" | rowspan=2 style="text-align:left;" | {{ill|Koku|en|Koku|qid=Q855048|lt=Koku|1=Koku}}{{efn|The ''koku'' has also appeared in English as the ''kokf'' and its multiples as the ''ikwankokf'' or ''ickmagog'' (1000{{nbsp}}''koku'') and ''man-kokf'' or ''managoga'' (10,000{{nbsp}}''koku'').}} | rowspan=2 align="center"| {{lang|ja|石}} | rowspan=2 | 100 | rowspan=2 | {{sfrac|240,100|1331}}{{nbsp}}L | rowspan=2 | 180.4{{nbsp}}L | rowspan=2 | {{sfrac|468,945,312,500|1,987,543,976,517}}{{nbsp}}cu{{nbsp}}yd | 47.65{{nbsp}}[[:en:US liquid gallon|gal]] | rowspan=2 | {{sfrac|24,010,000,000|605,084,579}}{{nbsp}}{{ill|Imperial gallon|en|Imperial gallon|qid=Q23925410|lt=gal|1=Imperial gallon}} | rowspan=2 | 39.680{{nbsp}}{{ill|Imperial gallon|en|Imperial gallon|qid=Q23925410|lt=gal|1=Imperial gallon}} |- style="text-align: right" | 5.119{{nbsp}}[[:en:US bushel|bu]] |- |colspan="13" bgcolor=#F0F0F0|'''Notes:''' * Approximations are rounded to four significant figures. |} {{anchor|Mass}} ==Mass== [[File:Gotou-fundou-10.jpg|thumb|A set of ten traditional Japanese ''fundō'' weights, used by money changers to weigh coinage. Top row from left are 30 ''ryō'' (1124.66 g), 20 ''ryō'' (749.07 g) and 10 ''ryō'' (374.62 g, twice), bottom row from left are 3 ''momme'' (11.19 g), 1 ''ryō'' (37.47 g, twice), 2 ''ryō'' (74.89 g), 3 ''ryō'' (112.42 g) and 4 ''ryō'' (149.77 g). All metric weights actual, not rounded.]] The base unit of Japanese mass is the ''kan'', although the ''momme'' is more common. It is a recognised unit in the international pearl industry.{{cite web|title=What is a Pearl Momme?|url=https://www.winterson.co.uk/blog/2011/04/what-is-a-pearl-momme/|date=April 2004|author=Winterson Limited|access-date=7 February 2019|quote=For these larger lots, pearls are sold by mass and the unit commonly used is the ''momme'', a traditional Japanese unit equal to 3.75 grams. [...] For larger lots of pearls, auctioneers may use the ''kan'', which is equal to 1,000 momme. }} In English-speaking countries, momme is typically abbreviated as ''mo''. The Japanese form of the {{ill|Chinese units of measurement|en|Chinese units of measurement|qid=Q435922|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese units of measurement}} [[:en:Tael|Tael]] was the ''{{ill|Ryō|en|Ryō|qid=Q3057942|lt=Ryō|1=Ryō}}'' ({{lang|ja|両}}).{{efn|The ''ryō'' is sometimes written in English as ryo, without its [[macron (diacritic)|macron]].{{sfn|''OED''|loc=[http://www.oed.com/view/Entry/169267 "ryo, ''n.''"]}} It also appears in English sources as the [[:en:Tael|Tael]],{{sfn|''OED''|loc=[http://www.oed.com/view/Entry/196989 "tael, ''n.''"]}} the ''táīl'', the ''táhil'', and the ''táïl''.}} It was customarily reckoned as around 4 or 10 momme but, because of its importance as a fundamental unit of the silver and gold {{ill|Bullion|en|Bullion|qid=Q557709|lt=Bullion|1=Bullion}} used as {{ill|Japanese currency|en|Japanese currency|qid=Q6158487|lt=currency|1=Japanese currency}} in medieval Japan, it varied over time and location from those notional values. {| class="wikitable" |+ Table of units of mass !colspan="2"|Unit !rowspan="2"|Kan{{sfnp|Nagase-Reimer|2016|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=SnDsCgAAQBAJ&pg=PR13 xiii]}} !colspan="2"|Metric !colspan="2"|[[:en:US customary units|US]] & {{ill|Imperial units|en|Imperial units|qid=Q641227|lt=Imperial|1=Imperial units}} |- ![[:en:Hepburn romanisation|Romanised]] !{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|qid=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}} !Legal !Decimal !Exact !Approx. |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | Mō |align="center"| {{lang|ja|毛}} or {{lang|ja|毫}} |height="30pt"| {{frac|1,000,000}} | {{sfrac|3|800,000}}{{nbsp}}kg | 3.75{{nbsp}}mg | {{sfrac|375|45,359,237}}{{nbsp}}[[:en:Avoirdupois pound|lb]] | 8.267{{nbsp}}{{ill|Micropound|en|Micropound|qid=Q30694162|lt=μlb|1=Micropound}} |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | Rin |align="center"| {{lang|ja|厘}} |height="30pt"| {{frac|100,000}} | {{sfrac|3|80,000}}{{nbsp}}kg | 37.5{{nbsp}}mg | {{sfrac|3750|45,359,237}}{{nbsp}}[[:en:Avoirdupois pound|lb]] | 0.5787{{nbsp}}{{ill|Grain (unit)|en|Grain (unit)|qid=Q693944|lt=gr|1=Grain (unit)}} |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | Fun{{efn|The ''fun'' is more often known in English as the candareen.{{sfn|''OED''|loc=[http://www.oed.com/view/Entry/26954 "candareen, ''n.''"]}} It also sometimes appears as the ''kondúrí'' or ''konderi''.}} |align="center"| {{lang|ja|分}} |height="30pt"| {{frac|10,000}} | {{sfrac|3|8000}}{{nbsp}}kg | 375{{nbsp}}mg | {{sfrac|37,500|45,359,237}}{{nbsp}}[[:en:Avoirdupois pound|lb]] | 5.787{{nbsp}}{{ill|Grain (unit)|en|Grain (unit)|qid=Q693944|lt=gr|1=Grain (unit)}} |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" |{{anchor|Momme}} [[Momme (unit)|Momme]]
Monme{{efn|Although ''monme'' is the [[:en:Revised Hepburn romanization|Revised Hepburn romanization]] of the unit, momme is more common in English.{{sfn|''OED''|loc=[http://www.oed.com/view/Entry/121027 "momme, ''n.''"]}} It also sometimes appears as the ''mommé'', ''me'', or ''mas''.}} |align="center"| {{lang|ja|匁}} | {{frac|1000}} | {{sfrac|3|800}}{{nbsp}}kg | 3.75{{nbsp}}[[:en:Gram (unit)|g]] | {{sfrac|375,000|45,359,237}}{{nbsp}}[[:en:Avoirdupois pound|lb]] | 2.116{{nbsp}}[[:en:Avoirdupois dram|dr]] |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | Hyakume |align="center"| {{lang|ja|百目}} | {{frac|10}} | {{sfrac|3|8}}{{nbsp}}kg | 375{{nbsp}}[[:en:Gram (unit)|g]] | {{sfrac|37,500,000|45,359,237}}{{nbsp}}[[:en:Avoirdupois pound|lb]] | 13.23{{nbsp}}[[:en:Avoirdupois ounce|oz]] |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | Kin{{efn|Particularly in historical sources, the ''kin'' is more commonly known in English as the [[:en:Catty (unit)|catty]]{{sfn|''OED''|loc=[http://www.oed.com/view/Entry/29045 "catty, ''n.¹''"]}} or ''katí''.}} |align="center"| {{lang|ja|斤}} | {{frac|4|25}} | {{sfrac|3|5}}{{nbsp}}kg | 600{{nbsp}}[[:en:Gram (unit)|g]] | {{sfrac|60,000,000|45,359,237}}{{nbsp}}[[:en:Avoirdupois pound|lb]] | 1.323{{nbsp}}[[:en:Avoirdupois pound|lb]] |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | Kan(me){{efn|The ''kan'' is also sometimes known in English as the ''kwan''.}} |align="center"| {{lang|ja|貫}}({{lang|ja|}}) | 1 | {{sfrac|15|4}}{{nbsp}}kg | 3.75{{nbsp}}kg | {{sfrac|375,000,000|45,359,237}}{{nbsp}}[[:en:Avoirdupois pound|lb]] | 8.267{{nbsp}}[[:en:Avoirdupois pound|lb]] |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | Maru |align="center"| {{lang|ja|丸}} | 8 | colspan=2 style="text-align:center;" | 30{{nbsp}}kg | {{sfrac|3,000,000,000|45,359,237}}{{nbsp}}[[:en:Avoirdupois pound|lb]] | 66.14{{nbsp}}[[:en:Avoirdupois pound|lb]] |- style="text-align: right" | style="text-align:left;" | Tan{{efn|Particularly in historical sources, the ''tan'' is more commonly known in English as the [[:en:Picul (unit)|picul]],{{sfn|''OED''|loc=[http://www.oed.com/view/Entry/143522 "picul, ''n.''"]}} ''pikul'', or ''pikel''.}} |align="center"| {{lang|ja|担}} or {{lang|ja|擔}} | 16 | colspan=2 style="text-align:center;" | 60{{nbsp}}kg | {{sfrac|6,000,000,000|45,359,237}}{{nbsp}}[[:en:Avoirdupois pound|lb]] | 132.3{{nbsp}}[[:en:Avoirdupois pound|lb]] |- |colspan="13" bgcolor=#F0F0F0|'''Notes:''' * Exact figures follow the 1891 Law of Weights & Measures and 1959 [[:en:International Yard and Pound Agreement|International Yard and Pound Agreement]]. * Metric values are exact. US & imperial approximations are rounded to four significant figures. |} ==Imperial units== {{ill|Imperial units|en|Imperial units|qid=Q641227|lt=Imperial units|1=Imperial units}} are sometimes used in Japan. Feet and inches are used for most non-sport bicycles, whose tyre sizes follow a British system; for sizes of [[magnetic tape]] and many pieces of computer hardware; for photograph sizes; and for the sizes of electronic displays for electronic devices. Photographic prints, however, are usually rounded to the nearest millimetre and screens are not ''described'' in terms of inches but "type" ({{lang|ja|型}}, ''gata''). For instance, a television whose screen has a 17-inch diagonal is described as a "17-type" ({{lang|ja|17型}}) and one with a 32-inch {{ill|Widescreen|en|Widescreen|qid=Q752245|lt=Widescreen|1=Widescreen}} screen is called a "32-vista-type" ({{lang|ja|32V型}}). ==See also== *{{ill|Japanese numerals|en|Japanese numerals|qid=Q1337084|lt=Japanese numerals|1=Japanese numerals}}, {{ill|Japanese counter word|en|Japanese counter word|qid=Q2116774|lt=counter words|1=Japanese counter word}}, {{ill|Japanese currency|en|Japanese currency|qid=Q6158487|lt=currency|1=Japanese currency}}, & {{ill|Japanese clock|en|Japanese clock|qid=Q928843|lt=clocks|1=Japanese clock}} *{{ill|Heavenly Stems|en|Heavenly Stems|qid=Q1190985|lt=Heavenly Stems|1=Heavenly Stems}} & {{ill|Earthly Branches|en|Earthly Branches|qid=Q1072961|lt=Earthly Branches|1=Earthly Branches}} *[[:en:Units of measurement|Units]], [[:en:Systems of measurement|Systems]], & {{ill|History of measurement|en|History of measurement|qid=Q206567|lt=History of measurement|1=History of measurement}} *{{ill|Chinese units of measurement|en|Chinese units of measurement|qid=Q435922|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese units of measurement}}, {{ill|Taiwanese units of measurement|en|Taiwanese units of measurement|qid=Q715103|lt=Taiwanese|1=Taiwanese units of measurement}}, {{ill|Hong Kong units of measurement|en|Hong Kong units of measurement|qid=Q5895496|lt=Hong Kong|1=Hong Kong units of measurement}}, {{ill|Mongolian units|en|Mongolian units|qid=Q30694057|lt=Mongolian|1=Mongolian units}}, [[:en:Korean units|Korean]], & {{ill|Vietnamese units of measurement|en|Vietnamese units of measurement|qid=Q4130073|lt=Vietnamese units of measurement|1=Vietnamese units of measurement}} *{{ill|Metric system|en|Metric system|qid=Q232405|lt=Metric system|1=Metric system}} & {{ill|Metrication|en|Metrication|qid=Q2738925|lt=Metrication|1=Metrication}} ==References== ===Notes=== {{Notelist}} ===Citations=== {{reflist}} ==Bibliography== * {{citation |ref={{harvid|''OED''}} Carpentry Museum] *[https://web.archive.org/web/20051211141235/http://homepage3.nifty.com/such/shumi/shumi3/shumi3.html Japanese units] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.sljfaq.org/cgi/junit.html Convert traditional Japanese units to metric and imperial units] ([http://www.sljfaq.org/cgi/junit_length.html lengths], [http://www.sljfaq.org/cgi/junit_area.html areas], [http://www.sljfaq.org/cgi/junit_volume.html volumes], [http://www.sljfaq.org/cgi/junit_weight.html weights]) (sci.lang.Japan FAQ pages)
